problem_statement.Title: --notes option ignores note tags that are entirely punctuation
problem_statement.Description: The `--notes` option in pylint fails to detect note tags that consist entirely of punctuation characters (e.g., `???`, `!!!`). When such tags are specified in the `--notes` configuration, pylint does not report them as fixme warnings (W0511).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Wrong assumption: The code in pylint/checkers/misc.py (lines 124 and 126) assumes note tags will always be followed by a word character, because the regex uses \b (word boundary) which requires a transition from a word character to a non-word character.
2. Location: pylint/checkers/misc.py lines 124 and 126 — the regex patterns for note tags (both for `notes_rgx` config at line 124 and default `notes` config at line 126) append \b to the pattern. For punctuation-only tags like ???, the colon `:` that follows is also a non-word character, so no word boundary exists after ???, causing the match to fail.
3. The hardcoded \b constant in lines 124 and 126 encodes the incorrect assumption about what can follow a note tag. The correct relationship should be a negative lookahead for non-word character (?!)[\w]) instead of \b.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python file test.py with the content:
Observe that only the first line triggers a W0511 fixme warning
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
Both lines should trigger a W0511 fixme warning, showing output similar to:
************* Module test
test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
test.py:2:1: W0511: ???: no (fixme)
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Only the first line triggers a W0511 fixme warning. The output shows:
************* Module test
test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
The line with ??? is silently ignored.

hints_text: Did a little investigation, this is we're actually converting this option in a regular expression pattern (thereby making it awfully similar to the `notes-rgx` option). Since `?` is a special character in regex this doesn't get picked up. Using `\?\?\?` in either `notes` or `notes-rgx` should work.

problem_statement.Title: Traceback printed for unrecognized option
problem_statement.Description: When an unrecognized command-line option (e.g., `-Q`) is passed to pylint, a Python traceback is printed after the E0015 error message. This is not user-friendly and differs from the clean error messages shown by similar tools like mypy.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. The `_UnrecognizedOptionError` exception is raised unconditionally at `pylint/config/config_initialization.py:85` after emitting the E0015 error message, allowing it to propagate through the call stack. 2. The call to `_config_initialization` at `pylint/lint/run.py:135-137` is not wrapped in a try-except block to catch `_UnrecognizedOptionError`, unlike the handling of `ArgumentPreprocessingError` at lines 112-114 which catches the exception and calls `sys.exit(32)`. 3. The `_UnrecognizedOptionError` exception class is not imported in `pylint/lint/run.py` — only `ArgumentPreprocessingError` is imported at line 16, preventing exception handling. 4. While `_UnrecognizedOptionError` is also raised at `pylint/config/arguments_manager.py:250` during config file parsing, that case is already handled at lines 55-60 where the exception is caught and a message is added without re-raising. The unhandled case is specifically when unrecognized options are passed on the command line.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: A clean, user-friendly error message displaying only the E0015 error (e.g., 'Command line:1:0: E0015: Unrecognized option found: Q (unrecognized-option)') without a Python traceback, similar to how mypy displays unrecognized arguments.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The E0015 error message is printed, followed by a full Python traceback showing the `_UnrecognizedOptionError` exception propagating through the call stack.

problem_statement.Title: --recursive=y ignores ignore-paths configuration
problem_statement.Description: When running pylint with the --recursive=y flag, the ignore-paths configuration from pyproject.toml is not applied. Files in directories that should be ignored (such as src/gen/*) are still being linted and producing errors.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. In pylint/lint/pylinter.py line 778 (_expand_files method), the code accesses ignore-paths via instance variable self._ignore_paths instead of directly from config. 2. self._ignore_paths is only set in the open() method at line 1014 using self.linter.config.ignore_paths, creating a confusing indirect reference. 3. This pattern differs from the correct pattern at line 604 (_discover_files method) which uses self.config.ignore_paths directly. 4. The instance variable approach creates potential timing issues where self._ignore_paths may be undefined or stale when _expand_files is called before open().
StepsToReproduce: Place Python files in src/gen/ that would normally produce pylint errors
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Files matching the ignore-paths patterns should not be checked by pylint, and no errors should be reported for those files.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Files in src/gen/ are still being linted and produce errors (e.g., empty-comment, line-too-long, invalid-name) despite matching the ignore-paths pattern.

@matusvalo I think I've run into a similar (or possibly the same) issue. Trying to reproduce with your example:

```
% cat src/gen/test.py 
import bla

% pylint --version
pylint 2.13.9
astroid 2.11.5
Python 3.9.13 (main, May 24 2022, 21:28:31)

## Fails for some reason
% pylint --recursive=y .   
************* Module test
src/gen/test.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
src/gen/test.py:1:0: E0401: Unable to import 'bla' (import-error)
src/gen/test.py:1:0: W0611: Unused import bla (unused-import)

------------------------------------------------------------------
```

EDIT: Just upgraded to 2.14.3, and still seems to report the same.
Hmm I can reproduce your error, and now I understand the root cause. The root cause is following. The decision of skipping the path is here:

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/3c5eca2ded3dd2b59ebaf23eb289453b5d2930f0/pylint/lint/pylinter.py#L600-L607

* When you execute pylint with `src/` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

* When you uexecute pylint with `.` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'./src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

In the second case, the source is prefixed with `./` which causes that path is not matched. The simple fix should be to use  `os.path.normpath()` https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.normpath

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
--- a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
+++ b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
@@ -82,8 +82,10 @@ def expand_modules(
             continue
         module_path = get_python_path(something)
         additional_search_path = [".", module_path] + path
-        if os.path.exists(something):
-            # this is a file or a directory
+        if os.path.isfile(something) or os.path.exists(
+            os.path.join(something, "__init__.py")
+        ):
+            # this is a file or a directory with an explicit __init__.py
             try:
                 modname = ".".join(
                     modutils.modpath_from_file(something, path=additional_search_path)
@@ -103,9 +105,7 @@ def expand_modules(
                 )
                 if filepath is None:
                     continue
-            except (ImportError, SyntaxError) as ex:
-                # The SyntaxError is a Python bug and should be
-                # removed once we move away from imp.find_module: https://bugs.python.org/issue10588
+            except ImportError as ex:
                 errors.append({"key": "fatal", "mod": modname, "ex": ex})
                 continue
         filepath = os.path.normpath(filepath)

problem_statement.Title: Linting fails if module directory contains a Python file with the same name as the directory
problem_statement.Description: When running pylint on a directory that contains a Python file sharing the same name as the directory (e.g., directory 'a/' containing file 'a.py'), pylint fails with an error attempting to load a non-existent 'a/__init__.py' file. This breaks module resolution and causes false positive import errors.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. In pylint/lint/expand_modules.py lines 93-94, the code unconditionally sets filepath = os.path.join(something, "__init__.py") when input is a directory, without checking if __init__.py exists. This encodes the wrong assumption that all directories have __init__.py, which is incorrect for Python 3.3+ namespace packages.
2. In pylint/lint/expand_modules.py lines 124-133, the code adds filepath to results without checking os.path.exists(filepath), causing non-existent a/__init__.py to be passed to the linter.
3. In pylint/lint/expand_modules.py line 134-136, the has_init check uses os.path.basename(filepath) == "__init__.py" which checks the path string rather than file existence, incorrectly setting has_init=True even when the file does not exist.
4. In pylint/lint/expand_modules.py line 140, os.path.dirname(filepath) is used to find Python files within a package, but when handling namespace packages (directories without __init__.py), if filepath were set to the directory itself rather than a synthetic __init__.py path, os.path.dirname would return the parent directory (".") instead of the actual package directory.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Running pylint on a directory should succeed regardless of whether an __init__.py file exists, compatible with Python 3.3+ namespace packages.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Running "pylint a" fails with error: "error while code parsing: Unable to load file a/__init__.py: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'a/__init__.py'"

hints_text:

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/config/argument.py b/pylint/config/argument.py
--- a/pylint/config/argument.py
+++ b/pylint/config/argument.py
@@ -99,11 +99,20 @@ def _py_version_transformer(value: str) -> tuple[int, ...]:
     return version
 
 
+def _regex_transformer(value: str) -> Pattern[str]:
+    """Return `re.compile(value)`."""
+    try:
+        return re.compile(value)
+    except re.error as e:
+        msg = f"Error in provided regular expression: {value} beginning at index {e.pos}: {e.msg}"
+        raise argparse.ArgumentTypeError(msg)
+
+

     """Transforms a comma separated list of regular expressions."""
     patterns: list[Pattern[str]] = []
     for pattern in _csv_transformer(value):
-        patterns.append(re.compile(pattern))
+        patterns.append(_regex_transformer(pattern))
     return patterns
 
 
@@ -130,7 +139,7 @@ def _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     "non_empty_string": _non_empty_string_transformer,
     "path": _path_transformer,
     "py_version": _py_version_transformer,
-    "regexp": re.compile,
+    "regexp": _regex_transformer,
     "regexp_csv": _regexp_csv_transfomer,
     "regexp_paths_csv": _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer,
     "string": pylint_utils._unquote,

problem_statement.Title: Using Unicode property escapes (\p{Han}) in pylint regex configuration throws re.error
problem_statement.Description: When configuring pylint with Unicode property escapes like \p{Han} in regex options (e.g., function-rgx=[\p{Han}a-z_][\p{Han}a-z0-9_]{2,30}), pylint crashes during configuration parsing with 're.error: bad escape \p at position 1'. This occurs because user-provided regex patterns are compiled using Python's re.compile() without the re.UNICODE flag, preventing Unicode property escapes from being recognized.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. In pylint/config/argument.py:133 (_TYPE_TRANSFORMERS), the dictionary entry "regexp": re.compile passes no flags when the type transformer is called by argparse, preventing Unicode property escapes from being recognized. 2. In pylint/config/argument.py:106 (_regexp_csv_transfomer), patterns.append(re.compile(pattern)) lacks the re.UNICODE flag. 3. In pylint/config/argument.py:114-120 (_regexp_paths_csv_transfomer), uses re.compile(...) without flags for path patterns. 4. In pylint/config/option.py:26 (_regexp_validator), return re.compile(value) lacks the re.UNICODE flag, affecting .pylintrc parsing. 5. In pylint/config/option.py:30-31 (_regexp_csv_validator), the function calls _regexp_validator which also lacks re.UNICODE. 6. In pylint/config/option.py:41-47 (_regexp_paths_csv_validator), re.compile(...) is called without flags for path patterns. 7. In pylint/config/option.py:106 (VALIDATORS dict), the lambda "regexp": lambda pattern: re.compile(pattern or "") omits the re.UNICODE flag. 8. In pylint/checkers/base/name_checker/checker.py:286 and 289, good_names_rgxs and bad_names_rgxs are compiled with re.compile() without flags.
StepsToReproduce: Add the line "function-rgx=[\\p{Han}a-z_][\\p{Han}a-z0-9_]{2,30}$" to the configuration
Observe the re.error: bad escape \p at position 1 exception
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pylint should accept Unicode property escapes like \p{Han} in regex configuration options and compile the patterns successfully without throwing an error.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint crashes during configuration file parsing with a traceback ending in 're.error: bad escape \p at position 1', preventing pylint from starting.

hints_text:
This doesn't seem like it is a `pylint` issue?

`re.compile("[\p{Han}a-z_]")` also raises normally. `\p` also isn't documented: https://docs.python.org/3/howto/regex.html
Is this a supported character?
I think this could be improved! Similar to the helpful output when passing an unrecognized option to Pylint, we could give a friendly output indicating that the regex pattern is invalid without the traceback; happy to put a MR together if you agree.
Thanks @mbyrnepr2 I did not even realize it was a crash that we had to fix before your comment.
@mbyrnepr2 I think in the above stacktrace on line 1858 makes the most sense.

We need to decide though if we continue to run the program. I think it makes sense to still quit. If we catch regex errors there and pass we will also "allow" ignore path regexes that don't work. I don't think we should do that.

Imo, incorrect regexes are a little different from other "incorrect" options, since there is little risk that they are valid on other interpreters or versions such as old messages etc. Therefore, I'd prefer to (cleanly) exit.
Indeed @DanielNoord I think we are on the same page regarding this point; I would also exit instead of passing if the regex is invalid. That line you mention, we can basically try/except on re.error and exit printing the details of the pattern which is invalid.

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/reporters/text.py b/pylint/reporters/text.py
--- a/pylint/reporters/text.py
+++ b/pylint/reporters/text.py
@@ -175,7 +175,7 @@ def on_set_current_module(self, module: str, filepath: str | None) -> None:
         self._template = template
 
         # Check to see if all parameters in the template are attributes of the Message
-        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
+        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(\w+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
         for argument in arguments:
             if argument[0] not in MESSAGE_FIELDS:

+    )

+    assert out_lines[1] == '{ "Category": "convention" }'
+    assert out_lines[2] == '{ "Category": "convention" }'

problem_statement.Title: Using custom braces in message template does not work
problem_statement.Description: The --msg-template option fails to correctly parse format placeholders when the template contains escaped braces ({{ and }}) for Python format strings. The regex parser treats escaped braces as regular placeholder delimiters, causing incorrect matching of format arguments.
RootCause: Incorrect regex assumption at pylint/reporters/text.py:178 — The pattern r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}" assumes all { characters start a format placeholder and does not account for Python's {{ escape sequence for literal braces. When template '{{ "Category": "{category}" }}' is processed, the regex erroneously matches the entire string between the outermost braces as an argument, producing '" Category": "{category}"' instead of extracting just 'category'.

Inconsistent regex pattern at pylint/reporters/text.py:185 — The removal regex r"\{" + argument[0] + r"(:.*?)?\}" uses a different quantifier (.+? vs .*?) than the findall regex, and since the argument is extracted from the broken line 178 logic, this removal targets the wrong content in the template.
StepsToReproduce: Create a simple Python file with a lint error (e.g., a = 1/0)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The template should output the dictionary with 'Category' as the key and the message category as the value, for example: { "Category": "convention" }
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The output shows only '" }' repeated for each message, indicating the format argument was incorrectly parsed as '" Category": "{category}"' and the template formatting produced malformed output.

hints_text:
Subsequently, there is also this behavior with the quotes
```
$ pylint test.py --msg-template='"Category": "{category}"'
************* Module test
Category": "convention
Category": "error
Category": "error
Category": "convention
Category": "convention
Category": "error

$ pylint test.py --msg-template='""Category": "{category}""'
************* Module test
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
```
Commit that changed the behavior was probably this one: https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/commit/7c3533ca48e69394391945de1563ef7f639cd27d#diff-76025f0bc82e83cb406321006fbca12c61a10821834a3164620fc17c978f9b7e

And I tested on 2.11.1 that it is working as intended on that version.
Thanks for digging into this !
